description: SafeDev detection rules. Add your own entries to extend.

rules.name: Suspicious eval() with dangerous content
rules.description: eval() called with suspicious content (base64, imports, shell commands). This pattern is commonly used to execute hidden malicious payloads.
rules.advice: Decode or inspect what is being passed to eval(). Legitimate eval() calls (like pandas.eval) don't use these patterns.

rules.name: Dynamic code execution — exec()
rules.description: exec() runs arbitrary Python code at runtime.
rules.advice: Check what string is being passed to exec().

rules.name: Base64 encoded payload
rules.description: Decoding base64 at runtime is a common technique to hide malicious strings from static scanners.
rules.advice: Decode the base64 string manually and inspect what it contains.

rules.name: Dangerous dynamic import — __import__()
rules.description: __import__() used to load a dangerous module (os, socket, subprocess etc). This is a common malware technique to hide what is being imported.
rules.advice: Check what the imported module is used for. Importing os/socket/subprocess dynamically is a red flag.

rules.name: Shell command execution
rules.description: Running shell commands can allow arbitrary code execution on the host machine.
rules.advice: Verify what command is being run and whether user input can influence it.

rules.name: Network socket — outbound connection
rules.description: Packages making outbound network calls could be exfiltrating data.
rules.advice: Check what URL/host is being contacted and whether it is documented.

rules.name: Suspicious install script
rules.description: Install scripts run automatically during package installation and are a prime attack vector.
rules.advice: Read the script in full before allowing it to run.

rules.name: Environment variable harvesting
rules.description: Reading environment variables can harvest API keys, tokens, and secrets.
rules.advice: Verify why environment variables are needed and what is done with them.

rules.name: Reverse shell pattern
rules.description: Classic reverse shell invocations — attacker gains remote shell access.
rules.advice: Treat this as a confirmed malicious indicator. Do not install.

rules.name: Hardcoded IP address
rules.description: Hardcoded IPs may point to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
rules.advice: Check whether the IP is a known legitimate service.

rules.name: Credential / secret keyword
rules.description: Hardcoded credentials in source code are a serious security risk.
rules.advice: Never hardcode secrets. Use environment variables or a secrets manager.

rules.name: Hex-encoded payload
rules.description: Long hex-encoded strings are commonly used to hide shellcode or malicious payloads.
rules.advice: Decode the hex string and inspect its contents.

rules.name: Compile and execute bytecode
rules.description: Compiling strings to bytecode and executing them evades most static analysis.

rules.name: Import of dangerous module
rules.description: These modules are rarely needed in normal packages and are often used for shell access.
rules.advice: Verify why pty or telnetlib is imported.

rules.name: curl / wget download and execute
rules.description: Downloading and piping directly into a shell is a classic malware delivery method.
rules.advice: Almost always malicious. Do not install.
